content:
**Naive Bayes** classifiers trace to **Bayes' rule** and **19th-century** probability work, but the **naive** conditional-independence shortcut made high-dimensional inference practical in the **1960s** for text and pattern recognition.

**Spam filtering** and **document classification** popularized **Multinomial** and **Bernoulli** variants. The **Gaussian** flavor — modeling **continuous** measurements with **Normal** class-conditional densities — became the standard choice for **real-valued sensor and biometric** features in **pattern recognition** texts (**Duda, Hart & Stork**; **Bishop**).

**John A. Quinlan** and early **ID3/C4.5** era work sat alongside generative baselines; **scikit-learn** (2010s) bundled `GaussianNB` with **variance smoothing** for stable finite-variance fits on real tabular data.

Modern pipelines often reach for **gradient-boosted trees** or **neural nets** first, but GNB remains a **fast, interpretable generative baseline** — especially teaching **Bayes + distributional assumptions** before discriminative models like **logistic regression**.

timeline.label: Bayes & inverse probability (1760s–1810s)
timeline.description: Bayes and Laplace establish posterior reasoning — foundation for all Naive Bayes classifiers.

timeline.label: Naive Bayes for pattern recognition (1960s)
timeline.description: Conditional-independence assumption makes multivariate Bayes tractable for classification and text.

timeline.label: Gaussian class-conditional models (textbooks era)
timeline.description: Pattern recognition canon pairs continuous features with Normal P(x_j|y) per class — the GNB recipe.

timeline.label: Library-era GaussianNB
timeline.description: sklearn and peers ship closed-form GNB with variance smoothing for production tabular baselines.
